product_strategy.description: Discovery and validation methodology — determines what to build before any code is written.

discovery.principle: Discovery is a filter, not a sales pitch. If clear ROI cannot be established, the project is a non-starter.
protocol: uncover-actual-problem: look past initial requests — the 'big pressing problem' often obscures simpler, high-impact opportunities
brutal-roi-honesty: do not proceed without clear, measurable return on investment
shadow-real-users: spend time watching actual employees work — hidden processes automatable with simple logic often offer the highest impact
quick-wins-first: select use cases that yield immediate results to build trust before tackling complex overhauls
red_flags: ai-for-ai-sake: no clear business problem → leads to pilot purgatory with no production path
no-success-criteria: without KPIs, the project will be perceived as failure regardless of technical quality
magic-expectations: expecting 100% accuracy on day one → will churn during inevitable iteration phase
restricted-data-access: security roadblocks stall development and kill momentum
discovery.breakage_definition: Look for processes that are painful, repetitive, costly, AND unfixed. If all four boxes are checked, there is a serious opportunity.

proven_use_cases: document-processing: automating extraction and validation against company standards
content-generation: structured, template-based creation for marketing or reporting
customer-support-agents: handling 80% of repetitive tickets with clear escalation paths for the 20%
internal-knowledge-assistants: RAG-based systems for proprietary documentation
data-extraction: transforming unstructured data into structured formats for downstream systems
use_case_selection_checklist: repeatability: is the task structured enough for automation?
human-escalation: is there a defined path for edge cases the AI cannot handle?
error-impact: what is the cost of a hallucination? (must be low for customer-facing, high-oversight for critical)
structured-rules: can success be defined by a set of logical rules?

accuracy_paradigm.v1_accuracy: 70-80% — frame as iterative development, not finished product
accuracy_paradigm.v2_accuracy: 90% — requires rigorous iteration with domain expert feedback
accuracy_paradigm.principle: Shift mindset from 'perfection' to 'iteration' — clients must be hands-on domain experts, not outsource-and-forget.

proof_of_concept.confidence: used when technical path is unproven
proof_of_concept.deliverable: local demo, script, or technical report

minimum_viable_product.confidence: used when solution is repeatable and proven

mvp_vs_poc.rule: Never blur the lines between a demo and a product.

proposal_framework: problem-statement: rephrase the friction in the user's own words
scope-boundaries: explicitly list what the system will NOT do
technical-approach: architecture diagrams showing how the stack solves the problem
success-measurement: specific KPIs for the first iteration
ongoing-costs: estimates for API usage, infrastructure, and maintenance
product_strategy.turn_it_off_test: If you turn the system off and nobody notices within an hour, it's not solving a real problem. The faster they call, the better your product.

hitl_as_deployment_strategy.principle: Human-in-the-loop is not a safety fallback — it is a deployment accelerator.
hitl_as_deployment_strategy.strategy: Identify the tricky 50% of edge cases requiring human judgment (high-value transfers, legal approvals, ambiguous intent). Automate the routine 50% immediately. This compresses time-to-market while maintaining risk management.
framing: Don't sell 'full automation' — sell '50% automation today with a path to 80%'.
The 50/50 split builds trust: the human sees the agent working correctly on routine cases before expanding its authority.
Every HITL gate is a data collection point — approved/denied decisions become training signal for reducing the human-required percentage over time.
hitl_as_deployment_strategy.cost_of_failure_assessment: When scoping automation, always ask: what is the cost if the agent gets this wrong? Low cost → automate fully. High cost → HITL gate. Unknown cost → HITL gate until you know.

delivery.description: High-velocity delivery methodology — how to ship fast, scope correctly, and maintain quality.

sprint_model.rationale: Deliver tangible value before internal bureaucracy has time to stall the project.
anatomy: day-1: kickoff call — align on communication channels and finalize KPIs
day-2: scaffolding — fork the standard project template, establish repo structure
days-3-8: core development — build workflows, LLM integration, quality iteration
days-9-12: internal testing — edge cases, error handling, UI polish
days-13-14: final demo and ship to production

scoping_discipline.principle: Fixed-boundary proposals prevent scope creep. Every proposal explicitly lists what the system will NOT do.
patterns: outcome-based-pricing: charge per sprint or outcome, not hourly — shifts value from time spent to results delivered
value-based-pricing: tie price to measurable business impact (tickets resolved, hours saved, revenue generated)
recurring-sprints: software is never done — sell recurring sprints for new capabilities and maintenance

communication.principle: Protect deep work — save meetings for kickoff and demo only.
patterns: async-updates: progress via Slack/Markdown reports, not daily standups
meeting-discipline: only kickoff call and final demo — no status meetings
error-resolution: Sentry → extract trace as Markdown → feed to AI → generate and review fix

rapid_fix_loop.description: Fix production bugs in minutes, not hours.
steps: 1. detect: Sentry alerts via Slack with production error
2. extract: copy error trace as Markdown
3. prompt: feed trace + relevant context into AI
4. execute: generate, review, and deploy fix immediately

pipeline_management.principle: After sprint 1, clients often disappear for 2-8 weeks to test internally. This is a cash flow killer.
mitigation: strategic-over-planning: assume 50% latency rate between sprints, fill schedule accordingly
pipeline-buffering: engineers move to internal tool development or waiting-list projects when clients go quiet

standardization.principle: Same stack, same patterns, same file structure across every project. If you know where everything is in one project, you know where it is in all of them.
benefits: a small team scales output 10x without proportional headcount increase
new projects start from a fork of the standard template — not from scratch
AI agents are more effective when codebase structure is predictable and consistent

scaling_philosophy.principle: Scale through software leverage, not headcount. Recruiting and training overhead causes profits to plummet.
scaling_philosophy.golden_era: The market hasn't caught up to AI-native efficiency yet — use this gap to maximize output with minimal team size.
recurring_revenue: recurring feature sprints for new capabilities
scaling_philosophy.long_game: Models change every month, but processes must remain stable. Swap underlying LLMs as they improve without reinventing the delivery model.

productization_path.description: The arc from local prototype to commercial product. Every application follows this progression.
stages: 1. local-prototype: script or notebook proving the core logic works with real data
2. containerized-service: Docker-based deployment with persistence and error handling
3. automated-delivery: pipeline runs unattended on a schedule, delivers output to users (email, API, webhook)
4. multi-tenancy: user accounts, individual profiles, isolated data — the product serves multiple customers
5. custom-presentation: web-based dashboards or landing pages alongside automated delivery
6. tiered-service: different frequencies, depths, or capabilities based on user tier or preference
productization_path.principle: The modular design (registry pattern, base class inheritance, repository pattern) supports rapid evolution through these stages. Each stage adds capability without rewriting the core pipeline.
